figures.caption: Fig. 12. Generalization performance of models, across domains with structured calibration gains. The JT, P-TDA, and DD-TDA models were trained on 45 domains and tested on 5 unseen domains.

Fig. 11. Comparison of the proposed methods with the JT and PT methods for the sparse gain calibration problem. The P-TDA/DD-TDA has comparable performance to PT, demonstrating the domain-adaptability aspect.

Fig. 1. Layers/iterations of different LISTA architectures: (a) Conventional LISTA, (b), (c) NA-LISTA. (b) Single layer of the P-DTDA model with input data {y,A} and domain parameter σ available during inference. (c) Single layer of DD-TDA model with only input data {y,A} available during inference. Note: Green blocks represent the data available at inference time, and Gray blocks are trainable parameters.

TABLE III PER-SNR RECONSTRUCTION METRICS AND OCR ACCURACY ON MNIST TEST SET (2,000 SAMPLES PER SNR LEVEL). OCR ACCURACY CEILING ON CLEAN IMAGES IS 97.50%. P-TDA AND DD-TDA ARE NA-LISTA VARIANTS. ↑ INDICATES HIGHER IS BETTER AND ↓ INDICATES LOWER IS BETTER.

Fig. 4. Qualitative MNIST-CS reconstructions across SNR domains. Columns show ground truth (GT) and reconstructions from JT (LISTA), Tail-LISTA (tight), DDIM (diffusion model) and the proposed NA-LISTA with P-TDA and DD-TDA.
Fig. 15. A comparison of NMSE against the number of parameters and FLOPS for different methods.
Fig. 8. Comparison of top (15× 15) of learned W2 matrices for different domains.
Fig. 7. Comparison of top (15× 15) of learned W1 matrices.
Fig. 9. Results for generalization experiment: The models (except PT) were trained on D1, D3, and D5 domains. Observation: DD-TDA generalizes well and performs better than JT models.

Fig. 2. Comparison of the proposed methods with the JT and PT methods for NA-LISTA for different SNR ranges. PT’s performances are better than JT’s for each domain. The P-TDA/DD-TDA has comparable performance to PT, demonstrating the noise-tunability aspect.
Fig. 3. Comparison of the proposed methods with the JT and PT methods for NA-LISTA for broad SNR ranges, averaged over 5 experiments.
Fig. 14. Comparison of the proposed methods with the JT and PT methods for Phase Retrieval with sparsity [10, 7, 4] and HR tol: 0.05.

Fig. 5. Qualitative MNIST-CS reconstructions at SNR −10dB. Columns show ground truth (GT) and reconstructions from JT (LISTA), Tail-LISTA (tight), DDIM (diffusion model) and the proposed NA-LISTA with P-TDA and DD-TDA.
Fig. 6. Comparison of learned W1 matrices for different domains.

Fig. 13. PRα,L() is the tunable model for sparse phase retrieval. Note: Green symbols represent the data available at inference time. Red symbols are trainable parameters, and blue symbols are fixed parameters.
